ML, REML, and Satterthwaite inference

theory-reml.Rmd

Covariance model

For covariance parameters η\eta, lmmix constructs

V(η)=ZG(η)Z𝖳+R(η). V(\eta) = ZG(\eta)Z^\mathsf{T} + R(\eta).

This decomposition and the use of structured residual covariance matrices follow the general Gaussian mixed-model framework described by Harville (1977) and Pinheiro and Bates (2000).

Variances and Cholesky diagonal elements are represented on a log scale. AR(1) correlations use the hyperbolic tangent. Compound symmetry uses a scaled logistic transformation that respects its dimension-dependent lower bound. Toeplitz correlations are obtained from partial autocorrelations, and unstructured matrices use a Cholesky factor.

Profiled likelihoods

For a given VV, the generalized least-squares estimator is

β̂=(X𝖳V−1X)−1X𝖳V−1Y. \widehat\beta = (X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}X)^{-1}X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}Y.

The profiled negative ML log-likelihood is

12{nlog(2π)+log|V|+(Y−Xβ̂)𝖳V−1(Y−Xβ̂)}. \frac{1}{2}\left\{ n\log(2\pi) + \log|V| + (Y-X\widehat\beta)^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}(Y-X\widehat\beta) \right\}.

The REML criterion adds log⁡|X𝖳V−1X|\log|X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}X| and replaces nn by n−pn-p in the normalizing constant. REML originates with Patterson and Thompson (1971); direct likelihood derivations are given by Harville (1977) and LaMotte (2007). Cholesky solves are used instead of explicit matrix inverses.

Satterthwaite degrees of freedom

For a contrast row LL, define

q(η)=LVar⁡(β̂)L𝖳. q(\eta) = L\operatorname{Var}(\widehat\beta)L^\mathsf{T}.

If g=∂q/∂ηg = \partial q/\partial\eta and Σ̂η\widehat\Sigma_\eta is the inverse observed-information matrix, the implemented approximation is the Satterthwaite approximation (Satterthwaite 1946):

ν=2q2g𝖳Σ̂ηg. \nu = \frac{2q^2}{g^\mathsf{T}\widehat\Sigma_\eta g}.

The gradient and likelihood Hessian are calculated with numDeriv. For a multi-row hypothesis, the covariance of Lβ̂L\widehat\beta is decomposed into orthogonal one-dimensional contrasts and combined with the Fai-Cornelius moment-matching formula (Fai and Cornelius 1996).

Random effects

After covariance estimation, the empirical BLUP is

û=GZ𝖳V−1(Y−Xβ̂). \widehat u = GZ^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}(Y-X\widehat\beta).

This empirical best linear unbiased predictor follows the mixed-model derivation in Harville (1977).

Fai, Alex Hrong-Tai, and Paul L. Cornelius. 1996. “Approximate f-Tests of Multiple Degree of Freedom Hypotheses in Generalized Least Squares Analyses of Unbalanced Split-Plot Experiments.” Journal of Statistical Computation and Simulation 54 (4): 363–78. https://doi.org/10.1080/00949659608811740.
Harville, David A. 1977. “Maximum Likelihood Approaches to Variance Component Estimation and to Related Problems.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 72 (358): 320–38. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1977.10480998.
LaMotte, Lynn R. 2007. “A Direct Derivation of the REML Likelihood Function.” Statistical Papers 48 (2): 321–27. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00362-006-0335-6.
Patterson, H. D., and R. Thompson. 1971. “Recovery of Inter-Block Information When Block Sizes Are Unequal.” Biometrika 58 (3): 545–54. https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/58.3.545.
Pinheiro, José C., and Douglas M. Bates. 2000. Mixed-Effects Models in s and s-PLUS. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/b98882.
Satterthwaite, F. E. 1946. “An Approximate Distribution of Estimates of Variance Components.” Biometrics Bulletin 2 (6): 110–14. https://doi.org/10.2307/3002019.
